food_name: Galbi Jjim (Korean Braised Beef Short Ribs)
aliases: கொரிய வேகவைத்த மாட்டு இடுப்பு எலும்பு (Galbi Jjim)

tamil: கொரிய வேகவைத்த மாட்டு இடுப்பு எலும்பு (Galbi Jjim)

food_subgroup: Braised beef dishes — cross-cut bone-in beef short ribs slow-braised with soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, ginger, Asian pear, sugar, mushrooms, carrots, daikon, chestnuts, and glass noodles (dangmyeon)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no dedicated concept for Galbi Jjim. Closest parent is a braised beef rib dish. No URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Korean braised beef short ribs (galbi jjim) with soy sauce, sesame oil, Asian pear, sugar, garlic, ginger, mushrooms, carrots, daikon, chestnuts, and glass noodles (dangmyeon). FoodEx2 code A00MJ used as closest parent for braised beef composite dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Galbi Jjim. A00MJ (beef meat dish) used as closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Galbi Jjim as of 2026-04-16.

main_ingredients: glass noodles (dangmyeon / sweet potato starch noodles)

serving.description: 1 portion (2–3 short rib pieces + vegetables + glass noodles)
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Galbi Jjim is approximately 350g, comprising 2–3 bone-in short rib pieces with braised vegetables (carrot, daikon, mushrooms, chestnuts) and glass noodles in the braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Beef short ribs, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 174032); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for glass noodles, soy sauce, sesame oil, and vegetables for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for the complete Galbi Jjim composite dish. Nutrient values are derived from USDA SR Legacy braised beef short ribs as the primary anchor, adjusted for the composite dish composition including soy sauce (sodium contribution), glass noodles (carbohydrate contribution), sesame oil, and vegetables. Values are consistent with the input record which was sourced from the NIAS Korea Food Composition Database (koreanfood.rda.go.kr). The NIAS database is not on the approved source list; therefore USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved primary anchor and values cross-validated against the input. Input values are retained where consistent with USDA-derived estimates.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Glass Noodles (Dangmyeon / Sweet Potato Starch Noodles) and Composite Braised Beef Dish Estimation — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Galbi Jjim exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite GI of approximately 38 is estimated based on: (1) dangmyeon (sweet potato starch glass noodles) GI ~35 per University of Sydney GI Database — the primary carbohydrate source; (2) soy sauce marinade sugars with moderate GI (~60–65); (3) significant protein (15.5g/100g) and fat (10.5g/100g) from beef short ribs substantially suppressing glycaemic response; (4) vegetables (carrot, daikon, mushrooms) contributing low-GI fibre. Weighted composite GI of ~38 is defensible and consistent with published analogous braised meat-and-noodle composite dishes. GL per 350g serving = (38 × 10.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 13.9g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (525mg/100g) from soy sauce braising liquid; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1,838mg sodium, approaching the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000mg in a single dish.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Galbi Jjim to a reduced portion of approximately 150–200g, avoid consuming the braising sauce, and balance the meal with potassium-rich foods; the dish's potassium content (275mg/100g) provides partial compensation but does not offset the sodium load at standard serving sizes.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load per serving (GL 13.9 per 350g) with low composite GI (~38) driven by dangmyeon glass noodles; carbohydrate from marinade sugars and glass noodles is well-attenuated by high protein (15.5g/100g) and fat (10.5g/100g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Galbi Jjim is acceptable for people with Type 2 Diabetes at a standard 350g serving given its low GI (~38) and moderate GL (13.9), but portion control is important — individuals should monitor total carbohydrate intake from the glass noodles and marinade sugars, and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice to keep the overall meal GL within target.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (4.2g/100g) and cholesterol (55mg/100g) from beef short ribs, combined with high sodium (525mg/100g) from soy sauce braising, present dual cardiovascular risk factors at standard serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Galbi Jjim to occasional consumption at a reduced portion (150–200g), request less braising sauce to reduce sodium intake, and note that the dish's zinc (4.0mg/100g), selenium (22mcg/100g), and anti-inflammatory ginger compounds partially offset cardiovascular risk; the saturated fat content (4.2g/100g) warrants monitoring within daily saturated fat limits of less than 20g/day.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beneficial anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger (gingerols, shogaols), mushroom beta-glucan, and high zinc (4.0mg/100g) and selenium (22mcg/100g) supporting immune modulation and joint cartilage synthesis.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Galbi Jjim is beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its ginger-based braising liquid providing well-documented anti-inflammatory gingerols and shogaols, combined with excellent zinc (4.0mg/100g) for joint cartilage synthesis and selenium (22mcg/100g) for antioxidant defence; the high sodium content remains a caution for those on anti-inflammatory medications that affect blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Galbi Jjim (갈비찜) is Korea's most prestigious braised beef dish — slow-cooked cross-cut bone-in short ribs that become fall-off-the-bone tender after a long braise in a complex sweet-savoury sauce. The preparation begins with soaking and parboiling the ribs to remove blood and impurities, followed by a 1–2 hour gentle braise in a sauce of soy sauce, sesame oil, blended Asian pear (for enzymatic tenderisation via bromelain-like enzymes and natural sweetness), sugar, garlic, ginger, and mirin. The extended braising renders the collagen in the short ribs into gelatin, creating the sauce's characteristic glossy, unctuous body that coats every ingredient. Shiitake mushrooms, chestnuts, daikon radish, and carrots braise alongside the ribs, absorbing the deeply flavoured sauce. Glass noodles (dangmyeon — sweet potato starch noodles) are added near the end of cooking, drinking up the remaining sauce into silky, savoury, slightly chewy strands. The dish is traditionally served at Korean holidays (Chuseok, Seollal) and celebratory meals, garnished with thin egg strips (jidan), pine nuts, and jujube dates. Nutritionally, Galbi Jjim provides excellent zinc (4.0mg/100g) for immune function and joint health, selenium (22mcg/100g) as an antioxidant, vitamin B12 (2.0mcg/100g) for neurological function, and anti-inflammatory ginger compounds (gingerols, shogaols). The composite GI is low (~38) driven by dangmyeon glass noodles, making the glycaemic load moderate (GL 13.9 per 350g serving) despite the marinade sugars. The primary clinical concern is high sodium (525mg/100g) from the soy sauce braising liquid, delivering approximately 1,838mg per standard 350g serving. Per 100g: 188 kcal, 15.5g protein, 10.5g fat, 10.5g carbohydrate, 525mg sodium.
common_user_queries: Korean braised beef short ribs nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central braised beef short ribs data and cross-validated against the input record (originally sourced from NIAS Korea Food Composition Database). The NIAS database is not on the approved source list, so USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved anchor with adaptation documented. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for dangmyeon glass noodles and composite dish modelling. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source entry covers the complete Galbi Jjim composite dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for the Galbi Jjim composite dish (beef short ribs + braising sauce + vegetables + glass noodles) is not available in USDA FoodData Central as a single composite entry as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the complete braised composite dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central for this preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace per USDA FoodData Central for braised beef short ribs; natural trans fats from ruminant beef (vaccenic acid) are present at trace levels below reporting threshold. 4. The original input source (NIAS Korea Food Composition Database, koreanfood.rda.go.kr) is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, braised beef short ribs, FDC ID 174032) is used as the approved primary anchor. Input nutrient values are retained where consistent with USDA-derived estimates for the composite dish. 5. FoodEx2 code A00MJ is used as the closest defensible parent for a braised beef composite dish; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Galbi Jjim. 6. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for dangmyeon (sweet potato starch glass noodles, GI ~35) as the primary carbohydrate source, modulated by high protein and fat content from beef short ribs. GL per 350g serving = (38 × 10.5 × 3.5) / 100 = 13.9.
